content.problemStatement:
You are given arrays AAA and BBB of length NNN each. Determine the number of good pairs.

A pair (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) (1‚â§i,j‚â§N)(1\le i,j \le N)(1‚â§i,j‚â§N) is said to be good if **all** of the following conditions are satisfied:


i<j;i \lt j;i<j;
Ai=Bj;A_i = B_j;Ai‚Äã=Bj‚Äã;
Aj=Bi;A_j = B_i;Aj‚Äã=Bi‚Äã;

Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.

Description of each test case:



First line contains an integer NNN - denoting the length of the arrays.
Second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶ANA_1, A_2, \dots A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶AN‚Äã - denoting the array AAA.
Third line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,‚Ä¶BNB_1, B_2, \dots B_NB1‚Äã,B2‚Äã,‚Ä¶BN‚Äã - denoting the array BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of good pairs.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: The only good pair (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is (1,3)(1,3)(1,3). Here,

1<31 \lt 31<3
A1=1A_1 = 1A1‚Äã=1 and B3=1B_3 = 1B3‚Äã=1. Thus, A1=B3A_1 = B_3A1‚Äã=B3‚Äã.
A3=3A_3 = 3A3‚Äã=3 and B1=3B_1 = 3B1‚Äã=3. Thus, A3=B1A_3 = B_1A3‚Äã=B1‚Äã.

Test Case 222: There are no good pairs.
Test Case 333: There are 333 good pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j). These are (1,2),(1,3),(1,2), (1,3),(1,2),(1,3), and (2,3)(2,3)(2,3).
Test Case 444: There are 222 good pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j). These are (1,2)(1,2)(1,2) and (3,4)(3,4)(3,4).
